lesson_title: Member functions returning references to data members

cards.text: A member function can return a {{c1::reference}} to a private data member, which allows the caller to {{c2::modify}} that member directly.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.code:
class Value
{
private:
    int m_value{5};
public:
    int& get() { return m_value; }
};

Value v;
v.get() = 10;
std::cout << v.get();
cards.explanation: get() returns a reference to m_value. Assigning to v.get() modifies m_value directly. The value is changed from 5 to 10.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.front: What is the main danger of returning non-const references to private members?
cards.back: It breaks encapsulation. External code can modify the private member directly, bypassing any validation or invariant enforcement the class might provide.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.text:
To provide read-only access via reference, return a {{c1::const reference}}:
const int& getValue() const { return m_value; }
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.concept: When to return by const reference from getters
cards.explanation:
Return by const reference when:
  • The type is expensive to copy (like std::string, std::vector)
  • You want to avoid the copy overhead
  • You want read-only access without modification
For cheap-to-copy types (int, double, etc.), return by value instead.
examples: const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }  // expensive
int getAge() const { return m_age; }  // cheap, return by value
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: It returns a non-const reference, allowing external code to modify the private member directly, breaking encapsulation.
cards.explanation: The getter should return const std::string& to prevent modification. Returning non-const reference defeats the purpose of making m_name private.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.front: What are the two const keywords needed for a proper read-only reference getter?
cards.back:
• const on the return type: const Type& (reference to const)
  • const after parameter list: indicates member function doesn't modify object
Example: const std::string& getName() const
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.text: Best practice: Getters should return by {{c1::value}} for cheap-to-copy types (fundamental types, enums), and by {{c2::const reference}} for expensive-to-copy types (class types like std::string).
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.question: Why is this getter implementation preferred?
cards.answer: It avoids expensive copy of the vector while preventing modification (const ref) and can be called on const objects (const member function).
cards.explanation: Returning const reference is efficient for large objects and maintains const-correctness throughout.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.front: Can you return a reference to a local variable from a member function?
cards.back: No! Never return a reference to a local variable. The local variable is destroyed when the function returns, leaving a dangling reference. Only return references to members that persist beyond the function call.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members

cards.front: When might you intentionally return a non-const reference to a private member?
cards.back: Rarely - only when you explicitly want to allow external modification and there's no validation needed. For example, in low-level data structures where performance is critical and encapsulation is less important.
cards.source: 14.7 - Member functions returning references to data members
